title: Cold borscht
subtitle: Chilled beetroot soup with soured cream and dill
excerpt: Cooked beetroot blended into a vivid pink soup with soured cream, cucumber, spring onion, and dill, served cold with halved hard-boiled eggs. The summer equivalent of the winter red borscht — light, sharp, and a startling colour.
sourceNotes: Cold beet soup is made across Eastern Europe under various names: Polish chłodnik, Lithuanian šaltibarščiai, Russian cold borscht. All share the same principle — cooked beetroot as the base, dairy (soured cream, kefir, or buttermilk) for body, and summer vegetables for freshness. The Lithuanian version is characteristically pink from kefir; the Polish version uses soured cream. This preparation follows the Polish-Lithuanian tradition, which is the most widely made and the most accessible with everyday ingredients.

recipe.freezeNotes: Do not freeze — the texture of the soured cream and vegetables is compromised on thawing.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make the day before serving and chill overnight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day before. The colour and flavour intensify with chilling.

content.text: Cold borscht relies on the colour and earthy sweetness of cooked beetroot tempered by the sourness of cream and the sharpness of lemon. The result should be vivid magenta-pink, intensely flavoured, and served properly cold — not cool, but cold, in chilled bowls. Dill, cucumber, and spring onion provide freshness and texture against the smooth base.

Cook the beetroot whole and unpeeled to preserve colour. Once cooked and cooled, the skins slip off easily. Vacuum-packed cooked beetroot works as a shortcut — choose plain, not the kind packed in vinegar.

If starting with raw {{beetroot}}, place whole and unpeeled in a saucepan, cover with cold water, and simmer for 40 to 50 minutes until a skewer meets no resistance. Drain and leave to cool until handleable, then peel under cold water — the skin will slide off.

Blend the cooked {{beetroot}} with 200 ml of cold water and two-thirds of the {{soured-cream}} until completely smooth. Pour into a large bowl. Stir in the {{lemon-juice}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Taste — the soup should be clearly sour as well as earthy and sweet. Stir in the {{cucumber}}, {{spring-onion}}, and half the {{dill}}. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours. Serve in chilled bowls with a spoonful of the remaining soured cream, the remaining dill, and two egg halves per bowl.

items.cause: Beetroot cooked too long before blending, or acid not added soon enough
items.fix: Add the lemon juice as soon as the soup is blended — the acid fixes the colour. Well-cooked beetroot that is not over-boiled should stay deep red-pink

items.cause: Not enough water in the blend
items.fix: Add cold water a tablespoon at a time and blend again until you reach a smooth, pourable consistency — thicker than water, thinner than yoghurt
Taste again straight from the fridge and add more lemon juice and salt. Cold soups need more acid and seasoning than they did at room temperature

content.text: Cold borscht is the summer version of one of Eastern Europe's oldest soups, appearing at kitchen tables across Poland, Lithuania, Belarus, and Ukraine when the weather is warm enough to want something cold. It is served as a starter or a light lunch, always with hard-boiled eggs and bread. The colour is unavoidable — there is no subtle version. This is a soup that announces itself as soon as it reaches the table.
